content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Comedies you can watch over and over that never stop being funny?

**Score:** 96
**Body:** Liar, Liar is that one for me. The jokes are so simple and yet so effective! Jim Carrey’s performance is a joy and I never grow tired of hearing his character Fletcher eff up a situation by unexpectedly saying what should’ve stayed an inside thought.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Been watching Blazing Saddles and Young Frankenstein since they were new and I still laugh all these yrs later
2. My Cousin Vinny
  - Positraction!!!  She earned the shit out of that Oscar. 


I could watch that every day. 

Hugs
3. Tropic Thunder

Idiocracy
  - Idiocracy is getting sadder to watch for me. Which also sucks.
4. Walk Hard. I watched it before work one day and then after work I watched it again.
5. The Big Lebowski and O Brother Where Art Thou?

Step Brothers

Tropic Thunder 

Trading Places, Coming to America, The Jerk
6. Hot Fuzz
7. My Cousin Vinny
8. **Airplane** - Leslie Nielsen has such a great straight face. 

**Uncle Buck** - I always wished that I had my own uncle Buck growing up, nostalgia aside the jokes still land.

**The Birdcage** - Hank Azaria is perfect in this 

ETA to add the The Birdcage

## 2. Need a movie that makes me go FUCK YEAH LIFE IS AWESOME!!

**Score:** 41
**Body:** Something which makes you realize that you are wasting your life living the way you are living right now 

You are missing out on so much and life can be so much better and you should live up to your fullest potential and live life to the fullest, exactly how you want to live it 

For me only 1 movie has ever made me feel this way 

And that is The Matrix

I really want to see another movie like that 

Feeling kind of down today 
I want to feel THAT again ykwim

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Secret life of Walter Mitty is the correct answer
2. Dude the Secret Life of Walter Mitty absolutely nails that feeling, especially if you're stuck in a rut right now. Ben Stiller's character goes from daydreaming about adventure to actually living it and it's so satisfying to watch

  
Also maybe check out Yes Man with Jim Carrey - bit cheesy but damn if it doesn't make you wanna say yes to everything and stop being so safe all teh time
3. Ferris Bueller's day off
4. The Truman Show

Y tu mamá también

Amelie


5. About time
6. Waking Life
7. Dazed and Confused.
8. # EVERYTHING EVERYWHERE ALL AT ONCE

\*It also has talking raccoons and butt-plug kung-fu...
9. Good Year
10. The Big Lebowski

## 3. Suggest me the best psychological thriller you've ever watched!!!

**Score:** 35
**Body:** Psychological thrillers are my favorite genre of movies. I have previously watched Black Swan, Shutter Island, The prestige and a few more. I want to watch a movie on similar concepts...

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Primal Fear
2. Memento
3. The Game (1997)
  - That was such a confusing movie but I have watched it
4. Arlington Road

Sleep Tight

The Vanishing (1988)
5. Not the best I’ve seen, but [The Machinist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Machinist?wprov=sfti1#) is definitely worth a watch. 

Edit: I’d add [Donnie Darko](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Donnie_Darko?wprov=sfti1) & [Vanilla Sky](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vanilla_Sky?wprov=sfti1) to the list as well.
6. Fight Club
7. Jacob's Ladder

Requiem to a Dream
8. Bound is one of the best written movies I have seen. It was made by the Wachowskis and it was the movie that allowed them to do The Matrix. Very tight plot with nobody acting out of character. Well acted too
9. Silence of the Lambs

## 4. Can you think of movies that are set entirely during a storm or a blizzard or even just constant heavy rain?

**Score:** 34
**Body:** They don’t have to be great movies; they just need that constant weather.  Rain, snow, wind, whatever.  I suppose it’s acceptable if there’s no weather at the very beginning and/or end, as long as the storm is present for the majority of it. 

“Identity” is the first one that comes to mind for me.  LOVE that atmosphere.  However, if you’ve seen it, you understand that movie is quite a rare find.  I’m even interested in movies taking place entirely indoors but you can HEAR the weather outside.  I’d appreciate some additional suggestions beyond my default choice “Identity”! Thanks!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Ice Storm
2. Hard Rain (1998)
3. Blade Runner
4. Day After Tomorrow
5. The Hateful 8 - great movie

If u got Netflix the extended version adds a lot to good stuff. Worth watching that one imo
6. Storm of the Century (Stephen King)
7. Pontypool!!! ❤️
8. The Perfect Storm, The Thing, The Black Phone 2
9. Snowpiercer
10. Does “The Shining” work?

## 5. Movies on loss of a partner

**Score:** 32
**Body:** Hi everyone, it’s Valentine’s Day and 2 weeks since my partner passed away. I’m grieving and I want to watch a movie that’s a bit cathartic. Our relationship lasted a little under 4 years and there were ups, downs, and everything in between. We had broken up and gotten back together a couple times and were actually on an off time when he got into his tragic accident. I’m 28 and he was 31. Are there any films you suggest? These could be romantic films, feel good films, or films on loss. I just want to spend this Valentine’s Day on the couch watching movies. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. -	The Fisher King
-	Good Will Hunting
-	What Dreams May Come

Robin Williams triple feature. Very sorry to hear and I wish you healing
  - I agree with What Dreams May Come
2. Ghost
3. Truly Madly Deeply 1990, Alan Rickman and Juliet Stephenson. Heart breakingly beautiful .
4. After Life is a fantastic TV series about grieving the loss of a spouse.
5. P.s. I love you is probably exactly what you are looking for.

I personally like 50/50 but it's not really about losing a partner.

Wishing you all the best.
6. Good Grief - Dan Levy is in it if you like him
7. Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind fits the bill.
8. There’s one starring Aubrey Plaza that I heard got pretty good reviews called “My Old Ass” I haven’t seen it personally but everything I’ve heard about it has been positive
9. Try:Somewhere in Time (1980)

Sorry for your loss...

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Wind River is a haunting, emotionally heavy film elevated by Jeremy Renner’s brilliant performance. It lingers in your mind long after the credits roll.

**Score:** 1709

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The line about his face painting was stuck with me for weeks. So well written

"what's with the shit painted on your face"

"This is my death face" 

"how do you know what that looks like"

" I don't know, nobody taught me" 


  - Yep that moment really stuck with me. Like hes just guessing, hoping he got it right
2. She ran 6 miles in the snow, that is a warrior. I doubt you’ll make it 600 feet. — little out of context but still sends shivers down the spine.
  - Your comment made me rewatch the scene and I teared up, fuck,  I want to watch this film again but its brutal.
3. Why you flanking me?
  - You see it???

You didn't see it.
  - I love that scene. Great tension build.
4. Taylor Sheridan needs to go back to writing movies because this and Hell or High Water are top notch cinema.
5. “Suffering’s hard on young men. They don’t have our practice.”
6. I went in thinking this movie was going to be eh. I left with my mind blown and became one of my many favorite movies.

## 2. Argue with the wall. Rogue One(2016) is the best Star Wars movie...

**Score:** 1330
**Body:** This movie gave me so much hope for the new Star Wars movies and then they released

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One trait i appreciate about the Andor series and Rogue, is that the Empire felt like a genuine threat. All the others made it seem like a well funded nuisance.
  - Competent bad guys are good for a story.
    - It's not even just that they're competent and legitimately threatening, but in Andor we see the gross incompetence of individuals in the system that ultimately lead to the downfall of the empire from within, well before the Death Star is even complete. It fucking rules.
    - Especially for, say, a WAR story.

Ben Mendelsohn was perfect as a menacing Krennic, and they didn't overuse the top tier characters like Vader or the Emperor to establish the oppressiveness of the empire. 

Just pure greed and ambition coupled with insecurity
  - Empire strikes back also had competent empire.

Revenge of the Sith they were not half bad either
2. Top 3 for me. 

Its the only sequel that has a reason to exist.

I heard a reviewer once say that its the first Star Wars movie to show the horror of war.
3. As someone whos not really a fan of Star Wars. I found Rogue One to be the only movie I genuinely really enjoyed.
  - Absolutely. I think that every Star Wars movie is entertaining (even the new ones) but Rogue One is the only genuinely good one.
    - Empire strikes back is legitimately good.
4. It does drag a bit in the middle, but the whole Scarif part is easily some of the best Star Wars has to offer.

## 3. Thoughts on The Hunt For Red October?

**Score:** 411

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The tension was palpable the whole glorious movie
  - One glorious ping
    - One glorious ping only, pleashe.
2. "I would have liked to have seen Montana"


Great movie, even with Connery's complete lack of voice acting.
  - When I think Russia, I think  Scottish man’s accent
3. The score is absolutely incredible,
cast is stacked, and Baldwin doing a Connery ipershonashun in one of the most tense scenes is hilarious.  It’s the best of the Clancy adaptations IMO.
4. ONE PING ONLY
5. My favorite part is Rick Ducommun's one scene: "Some turbulence, eh, Commander?! You don't like flyin', huh?! Oh, this is nothin'! You should've been with us five, six months ago! Ohhh, you talk about puke! We ran into a hail storm over the Sea of Japan, right?! Everybody's wretchin' their guts out! The pilot shot his lunch all over the windshield, and I barf on the radio! Knocked it out completely! And it wasn't that light weight stuff either! It was that chunky industrial waste puke! Hey, you want a bite?!"
6. I maintain that Alec Baldwin is the best on screen Jack Ryan
7. "Today, we shail into hishtory."

## 4. Watched Smokin' Aces recently, my wife was reluctant because the reviews are so bad. I had no idea how poorly recieved this movie was.

**Score:** 403
**Body:** I thought I was reading the reviews for the sequel ! I know that one sucked. The first movie was cheesey and I guess I can see why at the time people had some Tarantino copy-cats. But c'mon, the cast was amazing. The clash between the Tremor Brothers and Ben Afleck's crew is easily one of my favorite moments in cinema history. Very few movies have done that. And the humor is just spot on, Chris Pine ? Tommy Flanagan ? Jason Bateman ?! Not to mention Taraji Henson and her sniper scene. All side characters, all of them stole the show in the scenes they were in. 

Maybe I just like trash. But I do not think this movie deserves the scores it has

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This movie was so much ridiculous fun and I will not apologize.
  - Absolutely
  - love it. great score too. it’s worth it just fir the Chris pine and Ben death scene. 
  - The ending scene is also shockingly well done and well played by Ryan Reynolds
2. I love this movie. Jeremy Piven commits his ass off and in my opinion plays an amazing coked up criminal. His exchange with Common at the end is awesome.

Also Bateman was hilarious. The movie didn’t take itself too seriously, which I think was the right call.
  - Hey get your dick beaters off that. Still a quote I use to this day.
3. This was my first Joel Edgerton movie. To see how good of an actor/filmmaker he is. Makes his performance in this much more understated than it was, and it wasnt a very big part.
  - https://preview.redd.it/59eown2ohhjg1.jpeg?width=345&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=5722a19aa173a64bc94ec808f02a67298f776fe3


4. Ryan Reynolds before he started playing himself in every movie.
5. This is like bullet train fun

## 5. Why are people criticising movies before they are even released?

**Score:** 300
**Body:** Why are people so quick to dismiss or criticise a film before it’s even been released? It didn’t always feel this way. Lately, I’ve noticed a growing trend of people complaining about or attacking movies long before anyone has actually seen them. For example, there has already been a wave of negativity surrounding *The Odyssey*, despite it not being out yet. The same thing is happening with the upcoming Beatles biopics and filming has only just begun.

It makes me wonder why audiences are so quick to judge a movie based on early casting announcements, brief production updates, or even just rumours. Instead of waiting to see the final product, many people seem ready to form strong opinions immediately. Why has it become so common to criticise a film before it even has a chance to be seen?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Have you met Reddit?
  - Or, the internet?
    - Or, you know, people.
      - idk, I feel like people used to be a lot more normal about media consumption, at least in the sense that they only obsessed over things they \*liked\*.
2. Ragebait is profitable.

Thanks to social media, outrage is highly monetised.

The result is zero balance. No nuance. Everything is either abysmal or the greatest thing ever. Authentic balanced takes and level headed criticism are considered unengaging, boring and are no longer welcome. Hate is a powerful engagement tool, and engagement is money. If there's no culture war, then money won't be made.

The money must be made. The hate must flow.

The middle is dead.

All hail the Black Mirror.
  - I just realized the black mirror is a screen smh
3. Same reason you posted this.
4. Why are people so quick to hype up a movie even before it is released? Anticipation is just deeply rooted within our society, conditioned by our taste or perspective of things
5. Because that's how Sonic was fixed 
6. Because it's a visual medium and we can see it. Would I be allowed to criticize part of a song I heard before it was released?

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. just watched eyes wide shut

**Score:** 62
**Body:** incredible. this movie had me hooked from the first scene (👀) and truly intrigued like no other film had me, tom cruise crushed his role and was amazing. he was so ignorant to the true makeup of females that when he truly realises his wife has fantasies with another men it truly spikes his insecurity to a different level which he portrays perfectly. Kidman had amazing acting aswell like a mother who hides her emotions in front of their kid, seen in the last scene truly mesmerizing with great meaning. Bill goes onto wandering around trying to truly spike his masculinity while still having the thoughts of his wife in his head, Numerous amounts of times. the relationship turns truly toxic where nicole cheats in her “dreams” signifying she had been to one of those parties before and bill cheating in real life or his “daydream” even though he couldn’t actually do it, his inner conscious always stops him like when he went to the bar to visit his old friend instead of fucking an escort.

The parties signifying what the elites really do and half of them being pedophiles was a great message sent by Kubrick on how they all have sex cults and are fucked up morally, It may be a reach but the parties did look at lot like the rothschild parties lol.

The ending really proved the statement said above as the cycle repeats and the elites get what they want.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I can’t imagine watching this for the first time in the middle of this current news cycle. Horrifying.

I’m always a bit torn how I feel about the execution of the film (and there are obvious caveats there) but the atmosphere is kind of undeniable.

Have fun with your extended reading!
  - Thank u so much , yes i did kinda relate the movie towards the files recently , gruesome
2. Glad you had such a great viewing experience and enjoyed reading your thoughts. For me, most great films—and great art more broadly—serve as some sort of launching point into other questions. Kubrick asks where this dark unconscious side to us originates, and while the film explores the topic rigorously we don’t arrive at concrete answers, which I think resembles how these ambiguous topics present in real life. On top of that, every element of filmmaking is working in tandem with this film towards an overarching tone and world. It’s an obvious sign of a director at the top of their game.
  - So true well said, also kinda emotional how this was last film and his so called best, might be my fav movie oat

## 2. Arco is the kind of animated film that needs more support

**Score:** 26
**Body:** I know I'm in the afterglow of having just come out of the theater, but [Arco](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e4RrOe8IbI8) was incredible. Beautifully animated. Incredible world building. Likable characters. A very heartfelt story.

It's probably not a film for everyone. I can see young children not loving it as much as adults because it is a slower methodical film. This isn't content to babysit your kids, this is art. It puts 99.9% of current releases(animated or live action) to shame as something takes influences from the past while pushing the medium of animation forward.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’ll weigh in just cause I feel like a film like Arco could use the discourse. I thought it was good, not great. 

The arc of the story felt off, it abruptly came to an end and I wasn’t sure it had earned the payoff. Like the ending should’ve been devastating but it kind of just missed me. Cool idea but emotionally I just didn’t feel it. The three stooges characters were probably my favorite part but they were really underdeveloped to the point where it ended up being confusing by the end of the film. 

And the animation was ok? Seemed like a Miyazaki film with less detail. I thought K-Pop Demon Hunters was more visually interesting. But most of this is personal preference. And I agree that animation that’s primarily targeted towards young adults / adults needs more attention so I was happy to go see it!
  - Totally get where you’re coming from

 A film's emotional impact can really hinge on the execution of the ending and character development. it’s tough when a cool concept doesn’t quite hit the mark.
  - I dont see what was confusing about the 3 stooges ?

Regarding what you said about the animation I feel this quite off. It’s like saying « yeah E. T is cool but the lighting is better in west side story ». Both Arco and Kpop are very différents films. Sayign Arco is Miyazaki with less détails is a bit harsh. It takes elements Ghibli, Moebius and Grimault art and then does its thing. Kpop plays with the low framerate and anime codes. But I feel Arco has a more « made by hand » approach that personnally touch me more.

Anyway everyone has his opinion. Arco was my favorite film of 2025. I wish I could wrige more but I’m not à native speaker and I’m fighting with the autocorrect
  - Gotta agree here. Arco has some great concepts visually represented, but its character arcs don’t really… go anywhere leaving it bereft of emotional resonance. Definitely enjoyed it still, but they left a lot on the table.
2. I really liked it and am looking forward to rewatching it. It reminded me of Ralph Bakshi except more warm and wholesome. It feels like the kind of movie that, sure you can criticize it, but it’s too idiosyncratic and personal feeling to poke at.
3. It my favorite film of 2025. It’s a simple yet pereftcly told story. 
It also does something that I struggle to find more and more in films where everything is connected : themes, arcs and staging.

It’s also imaginative visions that I don’t see often and yes you can see the influences but they are digested and become its very own thing.

That said, it’s a film that aims for feelings, if you don’t Connect with it, you will not enjoy as much as I did.

## 3. The real Hussain Ustara story is more insane than any film could capture

**Score:** 26
**Body:** With O Romeo releasing, I went down a rabbit hole on the actual people:

Hussain "Ustara" Sheikh:
- Only Mumbai don who refused Dawood's cartel
- Running CCTV surveillance in 1995
- Staff who could lip-read from distances
- Got nickname from surgical precision with blade

Sapna Didi:
- Husband killed by D-Company
- Planned to assassinate Dawood at Sharjah cricket match
- Weapons: umbrellas with hidden blades
- Stabbed 22 times when plan was leaked

Current drama:
- Ustara's actual daughter sued the film
- Claims the love story is FAKE
- Says they had sibling bond, not romantic
- Court dismissed her case Feb 7

The real history of Mumbai's underworld is fascinating. The 80s-90s era especially.

Anyone have book recommendations on this period?

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. Would you rather watch a film that swings big and misses, or one that’s carefully constructed but restrained?

**Score:** 12
**Body:** I just watched the second hunger games movie and I was sort of surprised with how dull the whole thing was.  The thing is, I couldn't quite point my finger at a performance, set piece or plot point and say 'there, THATS the problem'.  It was well executed and competent in everything it was reaching for.  Still, it was bleagh.

  
For some reason I found myself thinking of [Sweet Dreams](https://www.imdb.com/title/tt22399738/). While that movie wasn't particularly great, I could see what they were going for and there were enough genuine moments in the movie that it sorta stuck with me and comes to mind here and there. 

I don't quite have the idea fleshed out (which is why I post here) but I guess what I'm saying is Id much rather see a bad movie that fails in a particular way than a successful movie that just sort of does its thing and dissapears.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. For me it depends on my mood and mental capacity at the time.  I think I've seen enough movies that if my brain is geared up for it I want something that does something a little different and weird even if it doesn't completely land.
2. Setting aside the big-swing-and-hit ideal situations, there’s still room for both, and it situationally depends on the type of film (documentaries, for example, often-but-not-always benefit from rigor rather than flash). Further, there are some directors who can build something special out of meticulous craft rather than the big swing.

Now all that said, on average, I’ll certainly get more excited for the big swing.
3. I think most people who call themselves cinephiles would prefer the "swings big and misses" films, to the thuddingly competent ones. What I look for in any film is ambition, the desire to show something we've never seen before. It can't always work, but it's always worth a try. At least the failures are memorable.

I've only seen the first Hunger Games, and it was an absolutely competent 3-out-of-5 movie. It did exactly what I expected, and it did it to a good enough standard. It was fine. You could not pay me to watch it again, or to watch another one.

I think most people massively prefer competency though. They like knowing what to expect, and getting exactly that. That explains why the current biggest hits at the box office (Disney remakes and Avatar) did so well. However I would be surprised if those people called themselves cinephiles, in the same way that I'd be surprised if someone who only orders burger and chips calls themselves a foodie.
  - I agree but I also think a "true cinephile" might prefer the big swings but there's absolutely a place for movies that are just excellently made and have a good story. Like if you just love movies, what cinephile literally means, something like that is great too. Movies are art AND entertainment. It's ok to enjoy both, if anything it should be encouraged

Some examples off the top of my head: I'm Still Here didn't take any big swings but was excellently made in every level, brought me to tears three separate times, and told a super important political story. That's a 5 star movie and one of my favorites as a result. Meanwhile Resurrection was a movie I literally walked out of 3.5/5 of the way through. Huge swing that turns out boring, long, and is completely self indulgent and specifically trying to appeal to "cinephiles" in a way that makes it feel completely empty. 1 star movie and is a big swing.
  - Give me Southland Tales over something routine and formulaic any day
    - I'm glad I'm not the only one in the Southland Tales club.  

Is it good or coherent?  Nope.  Is it interesting and wildly ambitious?  Absolutely.
  - I really think that Hunger Games is not a good example, it's more than competent - it's pretty unique and talks about politics in a well articulated, surprisingly nuanced and entertaining way, and it also gets weirder with each episode. Making a political young adult film in my book is a big swing, not an obvious safe bet - even though the books were also popular before that - and it also doesn't miss.
4. Oh big swings all the way. I was fascinated by Megalopolis and still think about it, whereas far more competent movies left no impression. 

One of my favorite movies of 2025 was “The Testament of Ann Lee”, and it was mostly because of how fresh it felt compared to movies like “Sentimental Value” which I felt like I’d seen before. 

I’m also a big defender of “Tenet” because it’s such a big swing. And yeah, it whiffs in a lot of ways, but that doesn’t change the fact that it blew me away with its innovative approach to time travel.
5. The two primary things I judge a movie for are what it is setting out to accomplish versus how well it accomplished that goal. The former necessarily limits the latter, so I'd rather watch a big swing.
6. In general I would rather view the very messy but very brave "Megalopolis" than the meticulously executed "Tinker, Tailor, Soldier, Spy" because the adventurous spirit is more enjoyable than formal pedantry. 

## 5. First Time Watching Basic Instinct

**Score:** 6
**Body:** First off: loved it. Sharon Stone and Michael Douglas were superb!

My only question, and maybe it’s something I missed, is who was really obsessed with who between Beth and Catherine.

I hear there’s back and forth on if Catherine really was the killer. I’m of the opinion that she is 100 percent the killer and framed Beth, but I’m stuck on the conversation between Nick and the one officer he met with (the one washing his car) who told him how Beth’s husband was shot and there was talk of another woman, alluding to Beth possibly being gay. 

We’re lead to believe the other woman he’s referring to is Catherine, but I’m confused on how/why it was her. She definitely did not give a clingy/obsessive vibe, and what reason would she have for killing Beth’s husband, if she’s the one who cut Beth off back in college?

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


---
